question:
Refer to the advertising-monthly-sales data presented in Table 11.1. Consider the straight-line model, $E(y)=\beta_0+\beta_1 x$, where $y=$ sales revenue (thousands of dollars) and $x=$ advertising expenditure (hundreds of dollars).
a. Use the method of least squares to estimate the values of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.
b. Predict the sales revenue when advertising expenditure is $\$ 200$ (i.e., when $x=2$ ).
c. Find SSE for the analysis.
d. Give practical interpretations to $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$.
answer:
a. We used Excel to make the preliminary computations for finding the least squares line. The Excel spreadsheet is shown in Figure 11.5. Using the values on the spreadsheet, we find

$$
 egin{aligned}
 ar{x} & = rac{\sum x}{5}= rac{15}{5}=3 \
 ar{y} & = rac{\sum y}{5}= rac{10}{5}=2 \
\mathrm{SS}_{x y} & =\sum(x- ar{x})(y- ar{y})=\sum(x-3)(y-2)=7 \
\mathrm{SS}_{x x} & =\sum(x- ar{x})^2=\sum(x-3)^2=10
\end{aligned}
$$


Then the slope of the least squares line is

$$
\hat{ eta}_1= rac{\mathrm{SS}_{x y}}{\mathrm{SS}_{x x}}= rac{7}{10}=.7
$$
and the $y$-intercept is

$$
\hat{ eta}_0= ar{y}-\hat{ eta}_1  ar{x}=2-(.7)(3)=2-2.1=-.1
$$


The least squares line is thus

$$
\hat{y}=\hat{ eta}_0+\hat{ eta}_1 x=-.1+.7 x
$$


The graph of this line is shown in Figure 11.6.
b. The predicted value of $y$ for a given value of $x$ can be obtained by substituting into the formula for the least squares line. Substituting $x=2$ into the least squares equation yields

$$
\hat{y}=-.1+.7 x=-.1+.7(2)=1.3
$$


Thus, when advertising expenditure is $\$ 200$, we predict monthly sales revenue to be $\$ 1,300$. We show how to find a prediction interval for $y$ in Section 11.6.
c. The observed and predicted values of $y$, the deviations of the $y$ values about their predicted values, and the squares of these deviations are shown in the Excel spreadsheet, Figure 11.7. Note that SSE $=1.10$, and (as we would expect) this is less than the SSE $=2.0$ obtained in Table 11.2 for the visually fitted line.
d. The estimated $y$-intercept, $\hat{ eta}_0=-.1$, appears to imply that the estimated mean sales revenue is equal to -.1 , or $-\$ 100$, when the advertising expenditure, $x$, is equal to $\$ 0$. Because negative sales revenues are not possible, this seems to make the model nonsensical. However, the model parameters should be interpreted only within the sampled range of the independent variable-in this case, for advertising expenditures between $\$ 100$ and $\$ 500$. Thus, the $y$-intercept-which is, by definition, at $x=0 ($0 advertising expenditure)-is not within the range of the sampled values of $x$ and is not subject to meaningful interpretation.

The slope of the least squares line, $\hat{ eta}_1=.7$, implies that for every unit increase of $x$, the mean value of $y$ is estimated to increase by .7 unit. In terms of this example, for every $\$ 100$ increase in advertising, the mean sales revenue is estimated to increase by $\$ 700$ over the sampled range of advertising expenditures from $\$ 100$ to $\$ 500$. Thus, the model does not imply that increasing the advertising expenditures from $\$ 500$ to $\$ 1,000$ will result in an increase in mean sales of $\$ 3,500$, because the range of $x$ in the sample does not extend to $\$ 1,000(x=10)$. Be careful to interpret the estimated parameters only within the sampled range of $x$.

meta_info.caption: Applying the Method of Least Squares-Advertising-Sales Data
meta_info.section: 11.2 Fitting the Model: The Least Squares Approach
